nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs, rubble zones, sandy bottoms, seagrass patches and intertidal/subtidal flats; typically associated with gastropod shells onto which it transfers sea anemones (most often Calliactis polypus) as a mutualistic defence against cephalopod predators.

decorPreferences: large amounts of stable, well-anchored live rock that will not topple when the crab climbs or excavates
open sand or rubble bottom for foraging
dim or subdued lighting; the species is generally more active at low light levels
a selection of empty gastropod shells in progressively larger sizes for the crab to upgrade into as it grows
compatible host anemones (e.g. Calliactis spp.) for the natural defensive partnership, if available

diet.notes: Opportunistic scavenger and omnivore. Generally accepts a wide range of prepared foods including mysis shrimp, finely chopped seafood (raw shrimp, scallop, clam, mussel), silversides, sinking pellets and dried marine algae/nori. Documented to graze filamentous algae, cyanobacteria and detritus, but adults are predatory enough to take snails, ornamental shrimp, polychaete worms and small slow fish opportunistically. Iodine and trace-element supplementation is commonly recommended to support successful molts.

goodWith: robust fish that are too large or fast to be ambushed
tangs, larger wrasses and other peaceful but mobile reef fish that can hold their own
compatible Calliactis-type sea anemones intended as the crab's defensive partner
stable, well-aged FOWLR or invertebrate display systems
avoidWith: ornamental snails and other hermit crabs (will be killed for shells)
small ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy shrimp)
delicate sessile invertebrates within reach (LPS frags, zoanthid colonies, clams) that can be knocked, climbed on or nipped
small slow-moving fish that may be ambushed at night (e.g. small gobies, dragonets, firefish at adult crab size)
octopus and large predatory fish (triggerfish, large pufferfish, large wrasses) that prey on hermits
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: Large Indo-Pacific left-handed hermit crab famous for actively transferring sea anemones (typically Calliactis polypus) onto its gastropod shell as living armour. Hardy and beginner-friendly, but tank-mate-aggressive at adult size and best treated as reef-safe with caution.
careNotes: Distinctive Diogenidae hermit crab with mottled tan-and-cream carapace, red-banded white eyestalks and a markedly oversized left claw. Its hallmark behaviour is the active recruitment of sea anemones (most commonly Calliactis polypus in the wild) onto its gastropod shell: the crab strokes and taps the anemone until it releases its substrate grip, then transplants it onto its shell, and re-transfers the anemone whenever it upgrades shells. This partnership is primarily defensive against octopus predators; in captivity the symbiosis is interesting to observe but not strictly required for survival. Generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly to keep alive, but the practical care bar is raised by adult-size tank-mate aggression: like most large Dardanus species, adults will kill snails, smaller hermits and ornamental shrimp for their shells or food, and will opportunistically take small slow fish. Generally regarded as reef-safe with caution rather than strictly reef-safe — documented to climb on and dislodge frags and to occasionally nip at LPS polyps, although outright coral consumption is less common than in some other hermits. Provide multiple sizes of empty gastropod shells so the crab can upgrade as it grows; failure to do so is a commonly cited cause of cannibalism on snails. As with all marine invertebrates, highly intolerant of copper-based medications. Source spread: adult size 5-10 cm (LiveAquaria/BlueZoo cite 4 in (~10 cm), Wikipedia 10 cm max, reefguide.org 2-7 cm, Saltwaterfish 6 in (~15 cm) — averaged to 5-10 cm); minimum tank size 24-40 US gal across retailers, written here as 150 L (~40 gal) per the playbook's bias-larger rule for tank-mate-aggressive species; temperature 22-26 °C (72-78 °F across sources); specific gravity 1.023-1.026.
breedingNotes: Sexes are separate; females carry orange-pink egg masses on the pleopods which hatch into planktonic zoea larvae. Larval rearing is widely regarded as not practical in standard reef setups due to long planktonic duration, loss to filtration/skimming and demanding live-food requirements. Wikipedia notes the species has not been successfully reared in captivity at any meaningful scale; all aquarium specimens are wild-collected from the Indo-Pacific.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root resolved to this product page via search. Provided baseline husbandry parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12), care level easy, aggressive temperament, omnivorous diet, copper intolerance and iodine/trace-element supplementation recommendation.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked common name, adult size (4 in ~ 10 cm), minimum tank size (24 US gal), beginner care level, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with caution, Indo-Pacific range and the anemone-on-shell symbiosis. Disagreed with LiveAquaria on temperament (semi-aggressive vs aggressive) and reef-safety (with-caution vs no); averaged to semi-aggressive / with-caution.

additional.name: Reefguide.org (Florent's Guide to the Tropical Reefs)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Diogenidae, left-handed hermits), depth range (0-30 m), Indo-Pacific + Hawaii distribution, and a lower size estimate of 2-7 cm typical adult body (averaged with retailer 10 cm max into the recorded 5-10 cm).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Dardanus pedunculatus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Diogenidae, Decapoda, Anomura), full Indo-Pacific range (Seychelles to Japan and Hawaii, including eastern Australia), depth range 1-27 m, maximum size 10 cm, asymmetric left-claw morphology, octopus-predator defence rationale for the anemone partnership, and the note that this species has not been successfully reared in captivity.

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (40 US gal ~ 151 L; supported the bias-larger choice of 150 L for adult tank-mate aggression), temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.0-8.4, specific gravity 1.023-1.026, semi-aggressive temperament and reef-safe-with-caution rating. Outlier maximum size of 6 in (15 cm) was averaged out against the other sources.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Larger Dardanus hermit crabs molt less frequently than small ornamental shrimp; healthy adults are generally reported to molt every roughly 1-3 months, with juveniles molting more often as they grow. The crab usually retreats deep into its shell or a sheltered crevice for 1-2 days before and after a molt and is highly vulnerable in this window. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium and especially iodine and trace-element levels support successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most commonly cited cause of failed molts. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Forms no pair bond; mating is brief and females then brood eggs on the pleopods. Larvae are planktonic and have not been successfully reared at any meaningful scale in standard reef setups; all aquarium specimens are wild-collected from the Indo-Pacific.
